 {"id":"bd-1i1l","title":"Actor mailbox: bounded channel with backpressure","description":"# Actor Mailbox Implementation

## Goal

Implement bounded mailboxes for actors that provide backpressure and integrate with the cancellation protocol.

## Background

Actors receive messages through mailboxes. Unlike unbounded queues (which can cause OOM), bounded mailboxes provide backpressure: when full, senders must wait or get backpressure signals.

## Design

### Mailbox<M>
- Wrapper around bounded mpsc channel
- Capacity specified at actor spawn time
- Integrates with two-phase send (reserve→commit)

### MailboxConfig
- capacity: usize (default: 64)
- overflow_policy: OverflowPolicy (Block, DropOldest, DropNewest, Error)
- priority_levels: Option<u8> (for priority mailboxes)

### MailboxHandle<M>
- Sender half given to ActorRef
- Methods: send(msg), try_send(msg), reserve() -> SendPermit

### MailboxReceiver<M>
- Receiver half held by ActorCell
- Methods: recv() -> Option<M>, try_recv(), is_empty(), len()

## Backpressure Semantics

When mailbox is full:
1. Block (default): Sender awaits until space available
2. DropOldest: Remove oldest message, insert new one
3. DropNewest: Reject new message immediately
4. Error: Return MailboxFull error

## Cancellation Integration

- recv() respects cancellation checkpoints
- Pending sends are aborted cleanly on actor cancellation
- SendPermit is an obligation (must commit or abort)

## Priority Mailbox (Optional)

For actors that need priority message handling:
- Multiple internal queues by priority level
- High-priority messages processed first
- Prevents priority inversion

## Implementation Notes

- Build on existing channel primitives in src/channel/
- MailboxConfig stored in ActorCell
- Metrics: messages_received, messages_dropped, current_depth

## Testing

- Backpressure: verify senders block when full
- Cancellation: verify clean abort of pending sends
- Priority: verify high-priority messages processed first
- Lab runtime: deterministic message ordering

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Mailbox<M> with configurable capacity
- [ ] OverflowPolicy enum with 4 strategies
- [ ] Two-phase send integration (SendPermit)
- [ ] Cancellation-safe recv()
- [ ] Optional priority levels
- [ ] Metrics for observability","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-31T21:17:53.139171701Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-31T21:33:40.507956885Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["actors","mailbox","phase3"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1i1l","depends_on_id":"bd-3t9g","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-31T21:17:53.155566440Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1i1l","depends_on_id":"bd-trgk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-31T21:33:40.507927019Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-1jis","title":"Logical clocks: happens-before ordering for distributed events","description":"# Logical Clocks Implementation

## Goal

Implement logical clocks for ordering events in the distributed runtime, replacing wall-clock time with causally consistent ordering.

## Background

Wall clocks are unreliable in distributed systems:
- Clock skew between machines
- NTP adjustments can jump backwards
- No global 'now' across machines

Logical clocks provide:
- Happens-before ordering (causal consistency)
- Partial order on events
- Sufficient for most coordination

## Clock Types

### Lamport Clocks
Simplest logical clock:
```rust
pub struct LamportClock {
    counter: AtomicU64,
}

impl LamportClock {
    /// Tick for local event
    fn tick(&self) -> LamportTime {
        LamportTime(self.counter.fetch_add(1, Ordering::SeqCst))
    }
    
    /// Update on message receive
    fn receive(&self, sender_time: LamportTime) -> LamportTime {
        let local = self.counter.load(Ordering::SeqCst);
        let new = std::cmp::max(local, sender_time.0) + 1;
        self.counter.store(new, Ordering::SeqCst);
        LamportTime(new)
    }
}

#[derive(Clone, Copy, Ord, PartialOrd, Eq, PartialEq)]
pub struct LamportTime(u64);
```

Properties:
- If a → b (a happens before b), then L(a) < L(b)
- But L(a) < L(b) does NOT imply a → b (concurrent events)

### Vector Clocks
Full causal ordering:
```rust
pub struct VectorClock {
    /// One counter per node
    counters: HashMap<NodeId, u64>,
    
    /// This node's ID
    self_id: NodeId,
}

impl VectorClock {
    /// Tick for local event
    fn tick(&mut self) -> VectorTime {
        *self.counters.entry(self.self_id).or_insert(0) += 1;
        VectorTime(self.counters.clone())
    }
    
    /// Update on message receive
    fn receive(&mut self, sender_time: &VectorTime) {
        for (node, &count) in &sender_time.0 {
            let entry = self.counters.entry(*node).or_insert(0);
            *entry = std::cmp::max(*entry, count);
        }
        self.tick();
    }
    
    /// Check if this happened before other
    fn happened_before(&self, other: &VectorTime) -> bool {
        // self ≤ other and self ≠ other
        self.counters.iter().all(|(k, &v)| {
            other.0.get(k).map_or(v == 0, |&ov| v <= ov)
        }) && self.counters != other.0
    }
}

#[derive(Clone, Eq, PartialEq)]
pub struct VectorTime(HashMap<NodeId, u64>);
```

Properties:
- V(a) < V(b) IFF a → b
- Neither < means concurrent

### Hybrid Logical Clocks
Best of both worlds:
```rust
pub struct HybridClock {
    /// Logical component
    logical: AtomicU64,
    
    /// Physical component (bounded wall clock)
    physical: AtomicU64,
    
    /// Maximum tolerated skew
    max_skew: Duration,
}

impl HybridClock {
    fn tick(&self) -> HybridTime {
        let physical = wall_clock_ms();
        let logical = self.logical.load(Ordering::SeqCst);
        
        let new_physical = std::cmp::max(physical, self.physical.load(Ordering::SeqCst));
        
        if new_physical == self.physical.load(Ordering::SeqCst) {
            self.logical.fetch_add(1, Ordering::SeqCst);
        } else {
            self.physical.store(new_physical, Ordering::SeqCst);
            self.logical.store(0, Ordering::SeqCst);
        }
        
        HybridTime { physical: new_physical, logical: self.logical.load(Ordering::SeqCst) }
    }
}

#[derive(Clone, Copy, Ord, PartialOrd, Eq, PartialEq)]
pub struct HybridTime {
    physical: u64,  // Milliseconds since epoch
    logical: u64,   // Tiebreaker within same millisecond
}
```

Properties:
- Total order (unlike vector clocks)
- Bounded skew from real time
- Used by CockroachDB, YugabyteDB

## LogicalClock Trait

```rust
pub trait LogicalClock: Send + Sync {
    type Time: Clone + Ord + Send;
    
    /// Record a local event
    fn tick(&self) -> Self::Time;
    
    /// Update based on received message
    fn receive(&self, sender_time: &Self::Time) -> Self::Time;
    
    /// Get current time without ticking
    fn now(&self) -> Self::Time;
}
```

## Integration with Cx

```rust
impl Cx {
    /// Get logical timestamp
    fn logical_now(&self) -> LogicalTime;
    
    /// Tick for local event
    fn logical_tick(&self) -> LogicalTime;
    
    /// Update from message
    fn logical_receive(&self, sender_time: LogicalTime);
}
```

## Wire Protocol Integration

Every network message includes sender's logical time:
```rust
struct MessageEnvelope<M> {
    sender: NodeId,
    sender_time: LogicalTime,
    payload: M,
}
```

Receiver updates clock on every message.

## Lab Runtime

Lab runtime uses simplified logical clock:
- Deterministic counter (no wall clock)
- Same seed = same clock progression
- Virtual time mapped to logical time

## Choosing a Clock

| Clock | Size | Ordering | Use Case |
|-------|------|----------|----------|
| Lamport | O(1) | Partial | Simple coordination |
| Vector | O(nodes) | Total causal | Debug, conflict detection |
| Hybrid | O(1) | Total | Production systems |

Default: Hybrid for production, Lamport for lab testing.

## Testing

- Lamport clock increment/receive
- Vector clock happens-before
- Hybrid clock bounded skew
- Lab runtime determinism
- Wire protocol integration

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] LamportClock implementation
- [ ] VectorClock implementation
- [ ] HybridClock implementation
- [ ] LogicalClock trait
- [ ] Cx integration
- [ ] Wire protocol envelope
- [ ] Lab runtime compatibility
- [ ] Tests for all clock types","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-31T21:24:36.206463508Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-31T21:24:36.227124591Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["clocks","distributed","phase4"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1jis","depends_on_id":"bd-3sst","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-31T21:24:36.227077985Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-1jp7","title":"Blocking pool: regression tests for shutdown and thread management","description":"# Task: Blocking Pool Regression Tests

## Overview

After fixing the blocking pool bugs (shutdown_and_wait, thread handle joining,
spawn from handle), we need comprehensive regression tests to ensure:
1. Thread joins work correctly on shutdown
2. Active thread count is accurate
3. Spawning from BlockingPoolHandle works
4. No thread leaks under stress
5. Queue management is correct
6. Thread parking/waking works properly

## Bug Fixes Being Tested

### Fix 1: shutdown_and_wait thread joining
- Previously: Tried to join before shutdown, handles not accessible
- Fixed: Wait for active_threads counter to reach 0, then join handles
- Test: Verify all threads terminate within timeout

### Fix 2: Thread handles moved to BlockingPoolInner
- Previously: Handles in BlockingPool, not accessible from BlockingPoolHandle
- Fixed: thread_handles in BlockingPoolInner behind Arc
- Test: Verify spawning from both Pool and Handle works

### Fix 3: Active thread counter accuracy
- Previously: Counter could drift if spawn failed
- Fixed: Increment on spawn success, decrement on thread exit
- Test: Stress spawn/exit and verify counter matches reality

## Unit Tests

### Pool Creation Tests
- [ ] `test_pool_new_min_max` - Create pool with min/max threads
- [ ] `test_pool_new_zero_min` - Zero min threads allowed
- [ ] `test_pool_new_min_equals_max` - Fixed-size pool
- [ ] `test_pool_new_invalid_config` - max < min rejected
- [ ] `test_pool_default` - Default configuration works

### Shutdown Tests
- [ ] `test_shutdown_and_wait_empty_pool` - No tasks, immediate shutdown
- [ ] `test_shutdown_and_wait_pending_tasks` - Tasks complete then shutdown
- [ ] `test_shutdown_and_wait_timeout_respected` - Timeout triggers correctly
- [ ] `test_shutdown_and_wait_returns_false_on_timeout` - Return value correct
- [ ] `test_shutdown_all_threads_joined` - No zombie threads (verify via ps)
- [ ] `test_shutdown_idempotent` - Multiple shutdown calls harmless
- [ ] `test_shutdown_then_spawn` - Spawn after shutdown rejected
- [ ] `test_shutdown_wakes_parked_workers` - All workers wake on shutdown
- [ ] `test_shutdown_tasks_complete` - In-flight tasks finish

### Thread Spawning Tests
- [ ] `test_spawn_from_pool` - Spawn via BlockingPool::spawn
- [ ] `test_spawn_from_handle` - Spawn via BlockingPoolHandle::spawn
- [ ] `test_spawn_handle_clone` - Cloned handle spawns correctly
- [ ] `test_spawn_scales_up` - Threads grow when needed
- [ ] `test_spawn_scales_to_max` - Never exceeds max_threads
- [ ] `test_spawn_respects_min` - At least min_threads always running
- [ ] `test_spawn_under_load` - High-frequency spawning works
- [ ] `test_spawn_return_value` - spawn() returns handle or error

### Counter Tests
- [ ] `test_active_threads_starts_at_min` - Initial count equals min_threads
- [ ] `test_active_threads_accuracy` - Counter matches actual thread count
- [ ] `test_active_threads_on_panic` - Counter decrements on task panic
- [ ] `test_active_threads_on_spawn_fail` - Counter not incremented on failure
- [ ] `test_active_threads_shrinks` - Counter decreases when threads exit
- [ ] `test_active_threads_concurrent_updates` - No races in counter

### Queue Management Tests
- [ ] `test_queue_fifo` - Tasks execute in order (when single worker)
- [ ] `test_queue_length` - Queue length accurate
- [ ] `test_queue_unbounded` - Queue accepts many tasks
- [ ] `test_queue_empty_on_shutdown` - Queue drains before shutdown
- [ ] `test_queue_concurrent_push` - Multiple spawners safe

### Thread Parking Tests
- [ ] `test_worker_parks_on_empty` - Idle workers park
- [ ] `test_worker_wakes_on_task` - Parked worker wakes for new task
- [ ] `test_worker_idle_timeout` - Excess workers exit after idle time
- [ ] `test_worker_min_never_exits` - Min threads don't exit on idle
- [ ] `test_worker_park_notify_race` - No lost wakeups

### Task Execution Tests
- [ ] `test_task_result_returned` - spawn_blocking returns result
- [ ] `test_task_panic_caught` - Panic doesn't crash pool
- [ ] `test_task_panic_propagated` - Panic can be retrieved from handle
- [ ] `test_task_cancellation` - Cancelled tasks don't execute
- [ ] `test_task_priority` - Priority ordering if supported

### Thread Naming Tests
- [ ] `test_thread_names` - Workers have meaningful names
- [ ] `test_thread_name_prefix` - Custom prefix supported
- [ ] `test_thread_name_unique` - Each worker uniquely named

### Handle Lifecycle Tests
- [ ] `test_handle_outlives_pool` - Handle valid after pool dropped
- [ ] `test_handle_clone_independence` - Clones are independent
- [ ] `test_handle_spawn_after_pool_drop` - Behavior defined

## Stress Tests

```rust
#[test]
#[ignore]
fn stress_test_blocking_pool_churn() {
    // 10,000 tasks with varied durations
    // Verify no thread leaks, all complete
    let pool = BlockingPool::new(4, 16);
    let completed = AtomicU64::new(0);
    let rng = rand::thread_rng();
    
    for i in 0..10_000 {
        let c = completed.clone();
        let sleep_us = (i % 1000) * 10; // 0-10ms
        pool.spawn(move || {
            std::thread::sleep(Duration::from_micros(sleep_us));
            c.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed);
        });
    }
    
    // Should complete well within 30s
    assert!(pool.shutdown_and_wait(Duration::from_secs(30)),
        "Pool did not shutdown in time");
    assert_eq!(completed.load(Ordering::Relaxed), 10_000,
        "Not all tasks completed");
}

#[test]
#[ignore]
fn stress_test_blocking_pool_shutdown_race() {
    // Spawn tasks while shutting down
    // Verify: no panics, no leaks, deterministic behavior
    for iteration in 0..100 {
        let pool = BlockingPool::new(2, 8);
        let handle = pool.handle();
        let spawned = Arc::new(AtomicU64::new(0));
        
        let s = spawned.clone();
        let spawner = thread::spawn(move || {
            for _ in 0..100 {
                if handle.spawn(|| {}).is_ok() {
                    s.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed);
                }
            }
        });
        
        // Race: shutdown while spawning
        thread::sleep(Duration::from_micros(100));
        pool.shutdown();
        
        spawner.join().expect("Spawner panicked");
        
        assert!(pool.shutdown_and_wait(Duration::from_secs(5)),
            "Iteration {} failed to shutdown", iteration);
    }
}

#[test]
#[ignore]
fn stress_test_blocking_pool_counter_accuracy() {
    // Rapid spawn/complete cycles
    // Periodically verify counter matches reality
    let pool = BlockingPool::new(2, 32);
    let barrier = Arc::new(std::sync::Barrier::new(101));
    
    for _ in 0..100 {
        let b = barrier.clone();
        pool.spawn(move || {
            b.wait();
        });
    }
    
    // All 100 tasks waiting at barrier, counter should reflect this
    thread::sleep(Duration::from_millis(100));
    let active = pool.active_threads();
    assert!(active >= 2 && active <= 32,
        "Active threads {} out of bounds", active);
    
    barrier.wait(); // Release all tasks
    
    pool.shutdown_and_wait(Duration::from_secs(10));
}

#[test]
#[ignore]
fn stress_test_blocking_pool_panic_recovery() {
    // Many tasks that panic
    // Verify pool continues functioning
    let pool = BlockingPool::new(4, 8);
    let completed = AtomicU64::new(0);
    
    for i in 0..1000 {
        let c = completed.clone();
        pool.spawn(move || {
            if i % 10 == 0 {
                panic!("Intentional panic {}", i);
            }
            c.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed);
        });
    }
    
    assert!(pool.shutdown_and_wait(Duration::from_secs(30)));
    // 900 tasks should complete (100 panicked)
    assert_eq!(completed.load(Ordering::Relaxed), 900);
}
```

```bash
UNIT_EXIT=${PIPESTATUS[0]}

# Stress tests
echo ""
echo "[2/4] Running stress tests (timeout: 120s)..."
timeout 120s cargo test --lib --release blocking_pool_stress -- --ignored --nocapture --test-threads=1 2>&1 | tee "$LOG_DIR/stress_tests.log"
STRESS_EXIT=${PIPESTATUS[0]}

# Thread leak check with helgrind
echo ""
echo "[3/4] Checking for thread issues..."
if command -v valgrind &>/dev/null; then
    cargo test --lib blocking_pool_basic --release --no-run 2>/dev/null
    TEST_BIN=$(find target/release/deps -name "asupersync-*" -type f -executable | head -1)
    if [ -n "$TEST_BIN" ]; then
        timeout 60s valgrind --tool=helgrind --error-exitcode=1 "$TEST_BIN" --test blocking_pool 2>&1 | tee "$LOG_DIR/helgrind.log" || true
        HELGRIND_EXIT=${PIPESTATUS[0]}
    else
        echo "Test binary not found"
        HELGRIND_EXIT=0
    fi
else
    echo "Valgrind not available, skipping"
    HELGRIND_EXIT=0
fi

# Generate summary
echo ""
echo "[4/4] Generating summary..."

cat > "$LOG_DIR/summary.md" << EOF
# Blocking Pool Test Report

## Date: $(date -Iseconds)

## Test Results

| Suite | Status |
```

 {"id":"bd-1js3","title":"ActorContext: Cx extension with actor capabilities","description":"# ActorContext: Actor-Specific Capabilities

## Goal

Extend Cx with actor-specific capabilities, providing the interface actors use for all runtime interactions.

## Background

Actors need everything regular tasks have (cancellation, budgets, I/O) plus actor-specific operations:
- Self-reference (ActorRef to self)
- Child spawning (supervised children)
- Stopping (self and children)
- Parent reference (for escalation)

## ActorContext Design

```rust
pub struct ActorContext<'a> {
    /// Underlying capability context
    cx: &'a mut Cx,
    
    /// Reference to this actor
    self_ref: ActorRef<Self::Message>,
    
    /// This actor's ID
    actor_id: ActorId,
    
    /// Parent supervisor (None for root actors)
    parent: Option<ActorRef<SupervisorMessage>>,
    
    /// Children currently supervised by this actor
    children: Vec<ActorId>,
}
```

## Capabilities

### Self-Reference
```rust
// Get a reference to self (for giving to other actors)
fn self_ref(&self) -> ActorRef<Self::Message>;

// Get own actor ID
fn actor_id(&self) -> ActorId;
```

### Child Management
```rust
// Spawn a supervised child actor
async fn spawn_child<A: Actor>(
    &mut self,
    actor: A,
    mailbox_config: MailboxConfig,
    supervision_config: SupervisionConfig,
) -> ActorRef<A::Message>;

// Stop a child actor
async fn stop_child(&mut self, child: ActorId);

// Stop all children
async fn stop_all_children(&mut self);

// List current children
fn children(&self) -> &[ActorId];
```

### Self-Termination
```rust
// Request own termination (graceful)
fn stop_self(&mut self);

// Check if stop was requested
fn is_stopping(&self) -> bool;
```

### Parent Interaction
```rust
// Get parent supervisor reference
fn parent(&self) -> Option<ActorRef<SupervisorMessage>>;

// Escalate error to parent
async fn escalate(&mut self, error: Error);
```

### Delegation to Cx
```rust
// All Cx methods available via Deref or delegation
fn checkpoint(&self) -> Result<(), Cancelled>;
fn is_cancel_requested(&self) -> bool;
fn budget(&self) -> Budget;
fn now(&self) -> Time;
fn trace(&self, event: TraceEvent);
// ... etc
```

## Implementation Notes

- ActorContext holds &mut Cx internally
- Deref<Target=Cx> for transparent access to Cx methods
- Child list kept in sync with RuntimeState
- Stopping flag separate from cancellation (can stop without cancel)

## Lifecycle Integration

ActorContext methods interact with lifecycle:
- spawn_child() only valid in Running state
- stop_self() transitions to Stopping state
- is_stopping() reflects lifecycle, not just cancellation

## Testing

- Self-reference usable for tell() patterns
- Child spawning creates supervised relationship
- stop_self() triggers graceful shutdown
- Cx methods accessible through ActorContext

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] ActorContext struct with all fields
- [ ] Self-reference methods
- [ ] Child management methods
- [ ] Self-termination methods
- [ ] Parent interaction methods
- [ ] Transparent Cx delegation
- [ ] Integration with actor lifecycle states","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-31T21:19:56.050864288Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-31T21:33:50.702274444Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["actors","context","phase3"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1js3","depends_on_id":"bd-3dcp","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-31T21:33:50.702211998Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1js3","depends_on_id":"bd-3t9g","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-31T21:19:56.073383378Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1js3","depends_on_id":"bd-trgk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-31T21:33:48.602178888Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1kg0","title":"WebSocket handshake: HTTP upgrade negotiation","description":"# WebSocket Handshake Implementation

## Goal

Implement the WebSocket opening handshake (HTTP upgrade) for both client and server roles.

## Background

WebSocket connections start as HTTP/1.1 and upgrade via the Upgrade header mechanism.

### Client Request
```http
GET /chat HTTP/1.1
Host: server.example.com
Upgrade: websocket
Connection: Upgrade
Sec-WebSocket-Key: dGhlIHNhbXBsZSBub25jZQ==
Sec-WebSocket-Version: 13
Sec-WebSocket-Protocol: chat, superchat
Sec-WebSocket-Extensions: permessage-deflate
```

### Server Response
```http
HTTP/1.1 101 Switching Protocols
Upgrade: websocket
Connection: Upgrade
Sec-WebSocket-Accept: s3pPLMBiTxaQ9kYGzzhZRbK+xOo=
Sec-WebSocket-Protocol: chat
```

## Types to Implement

### ClientHandshake
```rust
pub struct ClientHandshake {
    /// WebSocket URL (ws:// or wss://)
    url: Url,
    
    /// Random 16-byte key, base64 encoded
    key: String,
    
    /// Requested subprotocols (optional)
    protocols: Vec<String>,
    
    /// Requested extensions (optional)
    extensions: Vec<String>,
    
    /// Additional headers
    headers: HeaderMap,
}

impl ClientHandshake {
    /// Generate the HTTP upgrade request
    pub fn request(&self) -> Request<()>;
    
    /// Validate server response
    pub fn validate_response(&self, response: &Response<()>) -> Result<(), HandshakeError>;
}
```

### ServerHandshake
```rust
pub struct ServerHandshake {
    /// Supported subprotocols
    supported_protocols: Vec<String>,
    
    /// Supported extensions
    supported_extensions: Vec<String>,
    
    /// Custom validation callback
    validator: Option<Box<dyn Fn(&Request<()>) -> bool>>,
}

impl ServerHandshake {
    /// Validate client request and generate response
    pub fn accept(&self, request: &Request<()>) -> Result<Response<()>, HandshakeError>;
    
    /// Reject with custom status code
    pub fn reject(status: StatusCode) -> Response<()>;
}
```

### HandshakeError
```rust
pub enum HandshakeError {
    /// Invalid HTTP request
    InvalidRequest(String),
    
    /// Missing required header
    MissingHeader(&'static str),
    
    /// Invalid Sec-WebSocket-Key
    InvalidKey,
    
    /// Invalid Sec-WebSocket-Accept (response validation)
    InvalidAccept,
    
    /// Unsupported WebSocket version
    UnsupportedVersion(u8),
    
    /// Protocol negotiation failed
    ProtocolMismatch,
    
    /// Server rejected upgrade
    Rejected(StatusCode),
}
```

## Sec-WebSocket-Accept Calculation

The accept key is computed as:
```rust
fn compute_accept_key(client_key: &str) -> String {
    use sha1::{Sha1, Digest};
    use base64::Engine;
    
    const GUID: &str = "258EAFA5-E914-47DA-95CA-C5AB0DC85B11";
    
    let mut hasher = Sha1::new();
    hasher.update(client_key.as_bytes());
    hasher.update(GUID.as_bytes());
    let hash = hasher.finalize();
    
    base64::engine::general_purpose::STANDARD.encode(hash)
}
```

## URL Parsing

```rust
pub fn parse_ws_url(url: &str) -> Result<(String, u16, String, bool), UrlError> {
    // ws://host:port/path -> (host, port, path, false)
    // wss://host:port/path -> (host, port, path, true)
    // Default ports: ws=80, wss=443
}
```

## Client Flow

```rust
pub async fn client_handshake(
    cx: &Cx,
    stream: TcpStream,
    handshake: ClientHandshake,
) -> Outcome<WebSocket, HandshakeError> {
    // 1. Send HTTP upgrade request
    let request = handshake.request();
    stream.write_all(cx, &request.to_bytes()).await?;
    
    // 2. Read HTTP response
    let response = read_http_response(cx, &mut stream).await?;
    
    // 3. Validate response
    handshake.validate_response(&response)?;
    
    // 4. Return WebSocket on success
    Ok(WebSocket::from_upgraded(stream, Role::Client))
}
```

## Server Flow

```rust
pub async fn server_handshake(
    cx: &Cx,
    stream: TcpStream,
    config: ServerHandshake,
) -> Outcome<(WebSocket, Request<()>), HandshakeError> {
    // 1. Read HTTP request
    let request = read_http_request(cx, &mut stream).await?;
    
    // 2. Validate and generate response
    let response = config.accept(&request)?;
    
    // 3. Send response
    stream.write_all(cx, &response.to_bytes()).await?;
    
    // 4. Return WebSocket and original request
    Ok((WebSocket::from_upgraded(stream, Role::Server), request))
}
```

## TLS Integration

For wss:// URLs:
```rust
pub async fn connect_wss(
    cx: &Cx,
    url: &str,
    tls_config: TlsConfig,
) -> Outcome<WebSocket, WsError> {
    let (host, port, path, _) = parse_ws_url(url)?;
    
    // 1. TCP connect
    let tcp = TcpStream::connect(cx, (host.as_str(), port)).await?;
    
    // 2. TLS handshake
    let tls = TlsConnector::new(tls_config)
        .connect(cx, &host, tcp)
        .await?;
    
    // 3. WebSocket handshake
    client_handshake(cx, tls, handshake).await
}
```

## Testing

### Unit Tests
- Accept key computation
- URL parsing
- Request/response generation
- Validation logic

### Integration Tests
- Successful handshake
- Rejected handshake
- Invalid key
- Subprotocol negotiation
- Extension negotiation (basic)

### Interop Tests
- Connect to echo.websocket.org
- Connect to local test server

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Client handshake works
- [ ] Server handshake works
- [ ] TLS (wss://) supported
- [ ] Subprotocol negotiation
- [ ] Extension negotiation (basic)
- [ ] Error handling complete
- [ ] Interop with standard servers

 {"id":"bd-293f","title":"Epic: Scheduler Performance & Fairness Improvements","description":"# Epic: Scheduler Performance & Fairness Improvements

## Overview

Beyond critical bugs, the scheduler has several performance and fairness 
issues that impact throughput, latency, and predictability under load.

## Background & Context

The scheduler's performance characteristics directly impact:
- **Throughput**: Tasks processed per second
- **Latency**: Time from task ready to execution
- **Fairness**: Equal treatment of equal-priority tasks
- **Efficiency**: CPU cycles per task

## Performance Issues

1. **PriorityScheduler O(n) insert** (`priority.rs:27-43`)
   - Linear scan for insertion position
   - Degrades with large queues

2. **Imprecise wakeup strategy** (`three_lane.rs:127-134`)
   - Always wakes first parker
   - No load balancing consideration

3. **Try-lock contention in stealing** (`three_lane.rs:367`)
   - Multiple stealers contend on same lock
   - Wasted CPU cycles

## Fairness Issues

4. **RNG tie-breaking bias** (`priority.rs:27-70`)
   - Single RNG hint used for all decisions
   - Predictable, non-random selection

5. **Duplicate scheduling across queues** (`priority.rs:131-135`)
   - Task can be in both local and global queue
   - Potential double execution

6. **Only stealing from ready lane** (`three_lane.rs:354-387`)
   - Cancel/timed work not stolen
   - Uneven work distribution

## Performance Goals

- O(1) or O(log n) operations on hot path
- Minimal lock contention
- Efficient work distribution
- Predictable latency

## Success Criteria

- Benchmark suite for scheduler operations
- Throughput improvement measurable
- Latency distribution documented
- Fairness verified statistically","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-31T20:58:38.139343833Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-31T23:52:50.883027533Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["runtime","scheduler"]}
 {"id":"bd-2aiq","title":"Sub-Epic: Message Queue Clients - NATS/Redis/Kafka Integration","description":"# Sub-Epic: Message Queue Clients

## Overview
Implements first-party clients for major message queue systems (NATS, Redis Pub/Sub,
Kafka) with full Cx integration, structured concurrency, and cancel-correct semantics.

## Background & Motivation
Anyone using Tokio will say 'I can use async-nats, rdkafka, redis-rs for message queues.'
We need first-party, cancel-correct alternatives that integrate seamlessly with
Asupersync's structured concurrency model.

## Key Differentiators from Tokio Ecosystem

### Cancel-Correct Consumption
In Tokio, if a task consuming messages is cancelled, messages may be lost.
In Asupersync:
- Message delivery is an obligation
- Consumption must be ack/nack/committed/aborted
- Cancellation drains in-flight handlers gracefully
- No 'message lost because task dropped'

### Backpressure as First-Class Protocol
- Explicit credit windows
- Bounded queues with budget integration
- Producer slowdown when consumers lag

### Structured Lifecycle
- Connection owned by region
- Region close gracefully disconnects
- Child tasks for message handlers scoped to region

## Message Queue Clients

### 1. NATS Client
- Pure Rust implementation (no C dependencies)
- Subject-based pub/sub
- Request-reply pattern
- JetStream support (durable streams)

### 2. Redis Client
- Redis protocol (RESP)
- GET/SET/HGET/etc. operations
- Pub/Sub subscriptions
- Streams (XREAD, XADD, XACK)

### 3. Kafka Client
- Producer with exactly-once semantics
- Consumer groups with commit offsets
- Transactional support
- (May wrap librdkafka for protocol complexity)

## Integration Points
- All clients use Asupersync TCP/TLS stack
- Cx checkpoints for cancellation
- Budget integration for rate limiting
- Tracing for observability

## Tasks in This Sub-Epic
1. NATS client core (connect, pub/sub)
2. NATS JetStream integration
3. Redis client core (commands, connection)
4. Redis Pub/Sub and Streams
5. Kafka producer
6. Kafka consumer
7. Message queue E2E tests

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] NATS client with pub/sub and request-reply
- [ ] Redis client with commands and pub/sub
- [ ] Kafka producer/consumer with commit semantics
- [ ] All clients cancel-correct
- [ ] Backpressure handling
- [ ] E2E tests with real services (Docker)
- [ ] Performance comparable to Tokio alternatives","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-01T01:23:22.858029369Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T01:23:22.871248277Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ecosystem-parity","messaging"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2aiq","depends_on_id":"bd-1gb7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-01T01:23:22.871213142Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-2c2i","title":"Lease manager: time-bounded resource claims","description":"# Lease Manager Implementation

## Goal

Implement a lease system for time-bounded claims on remote resources, preventing orphaned resources when nodes fail.

## Background

In distributed systems, resources can be orphaned when:
1. Client crashes without releasing resource
2. Network partition prevents release message
3. Client hangs indefinitely

Leases solve this: resources are only held for a bounded time. If not renewed, they're automatically released.

## Core Types

### Lease
```rust
pub struct Lease {
    /// Unique lease identifier
    id: LeaseId,
    
    /// Resource being leased
    resource: ResourceId,
    
    /// Holder of the lease
    holder: NodeId,
    
    /// When lease was granted
    granted_at: LogicalTime,
    
    /// When lease expires (must renew before)
    expires_at: LogicalTime,
    
    /// Lease state
    state: LeaseState,
}

pub enum LeaseState {
    Active,
    Expiring,  // Close to expiry, renewal needed
    Expired,   // Resource released
    Released,  // Holder explicitly released
}
```

### LeaseId
```rust
pub struct LeaseId {
    /// Resource ID
    resource: ResourceId,
    
    /// Unique grant nonce
    nonce: u64,
    
    /// Granting node
    grantor: NodeId,
}
```

## LeaseManager

```rust
pub struct LeaseManager {
    /// Active leases held by this node
    held: HashMap<LeaseId, Lease>,
    
    /// Leases granted by this node
    granted: HashMap<LeaseId, Lease>,
    
    /// Background renewal task
    renewal_task: TaskHandle,
    
    /// Clock source
    clock: Box<dyn LogicalClock>,
}

impl LeaseManager {
    /// Request a lease on a resource
    async fn acquire(&mut self, resource: ResourceId, duration: Duration) -> Result<Lease, LeaseError>;
    
    /// Renew an existing lease
    async fn renew(&mut self, lease: &mut Lease, duration: Duration) -> Result<(), LeaseError>;
    
    /// Release a lease early
    async fn release(&mut self, lease: Lease) -> Result<(), LeaseError>;
    
    /// Check if lease is still valid
    fn is_valid(&self, lease: &Lease) -> bool;
    
    /// Get time until expiry
    fn time_until_expiry(&self, lease: &Lease) -> Duration;
}
```

## Renewal Strategy

```rust
pub struct RenewalConfig {
    /// Renew when this fraction of lease time remains
    renewal_threshold: f64,  // e.g., 0.5 = renew at half-time
    
    /// Retry interval if renewal fails
    retry_interval: Duration,
    
    /// Max retries before giving up
    max_retries: u32,
}
```

Background task renews leases automatically:
1. Monitor all held leases
2. When time_until_expiry < threshold, renew
3. On failure, retry with backoff
4. If max_retries exceeded, trigger lease_lost callback

## Lease Lost Handling

When a lease expires without renewal:
```rust
pub trait LeaseObserver {
    /// Called when lease cannot be renewed
    fn on_lease_lost(&self, lease: LeaseId, reason: LeaseError);
}
```

Default behavior: cancel tasks/actors using the leased resource.

## Clock Considerations

Leases use logical time, not wall time:
- Prevents issues from clock skew
- Lease duration is 'messages' not 'seconds'
- Renewal piggybacks on normal communication

Alternative: Hybrid logical clocks for bounded skew tolerance.

## Wire Protocol

### LeaseRequest
```rust
struct LeaseRequest {
    resource: ResourceId,
    duration: Duration,
    holder: NodeId,
}
```

### LeaseResponse
```rust
enum LeaseResponse {
    Granted { lease: Lease },
    Denied { reason: LeaseDenied },
}

enum LeaseDenied {
    ResourceNotFound,
    AlreadyLeased { holder: NodeId, expires: LogicalTime },
    ResourceLimits,
}
```

## Integration Points

- RemoteHandle holds Lease for remote task
- ActorRef for remote actors has Lease
- Distributed regions use leases for membership
- Saga steps may acquire leases on resources

## Testing

- Lease acquisition and release
- Automatic renewal in background
- Lease expiry when holder crashes
- Multiple lease requests (conflict handling)
- Lab runtime with simulated network delays

- [ ] Lease and LeaseId types
- [ ] LeaseManager with acquire/renew/release
- [ ] Background renewal task
- [ ] LeaseState transitions
- [ ] LeaseObserver callback
- [ ] Wire protocol messages

### Queries
```rust
impl MySqlConnection {
    /// Execute query (text protocol).
    pub async fn query(&mut self, cx: &Cx, sql: &str) -> Outcome<Vec<Row>, MySqlError> {
        cx.checkpoint()?;
        
        // Send COM_QUERY
        self.send(Command::Query(sql)).await?;
        
        // Receive column count, column definitions, rows
        let column_count = self.recv_length_encoded_int().await?;
        let columns = self.recv_column_definitions(column_count).await?;
        
        let mut rows = Vec::new();
        loop {
            match self.recv_row_or_eof().await? {
                Some(data) => rows.push(Row::from_data(&columns, data)),
                None => break,  // EOF
            }
        }
        
        Ok(rows)
    }
    
    /// Execute prepared statement.
    pub async fn execute(
        &mut self,
        cx: &Cx,
        sql: &str,
        params: &[Value],
    ) -> Outcome<u64, MySqlError> {
        cx.checkpoint()?;
        
        // COM_STMT_PREPARE
        let stmt_id = self.prepare(sql).await?;
        
        // COM_STMT_EXECUTE
        self.send(Command::StmtExecute { stmt_id, params }).await?;
        
        // Handle result
        let affected = self.recv_ok_packet().await?.affected_rows;
        
        // COM_STMT_CLOSE
        self.send(Command::StmtClose(stmt_id)).await?;
        
        Ok(affected)
    }
}
```

## Goal

Implement comprehensive coverage metrics for schedule exploration, enabling users to understand how thoroughly their concurrent code has been tested.

## Background

Traditional code coverage (lines, branches) doesn't capture concurrency:
- 100% line coverage can miss race conditions
- Need metrics for scheduling decisions, interleavings, states

## Coverage Dimensions

### Schedule Coverage
```rust
pub struct ScheduleCoverage {
    /// Total schedules explored
    schedules_explored: usize,
    
    /// Distinct schedules (after normalization)
    distinct_schedules: usize,
    
    /// Estimated total schedules (if computable)
    estimated_total: Option<usize>,
    
    /// Coverage percentage (if total known)
    coverage_percent: Option<f64>,
}
```

### State Coverage
```rust
pub struct StateCoverage {
    /// Distinct states visited
    states_visited: HashSet<StateHash>,
    
    /// State space estimate
    estimated_states: Option<usize>,
    
    /// States per scheduling point
    states_per_point: HashMap<SchedulePoint, usize>,
}
```

### Race Coverage
```rust
pub struct RaceCoverage {
    /// All detected races
    races_detected: HashSet<Race>,
    
    /// Races explored in both orders
    races_covered: HashSet<Race>,
    
    /// Races explored in only one order
    races_partial: HashSet<Race>,
}
```

### Transition Coverage
```rust
pub struct TransitionCoverage {
    /// All state transitions observed
    transitions: HashMap<(StateHash, EventKind), StateHash>,
    
    /// Transition pairs (s1->s2, s2->s3 seen)
    transition_pairs: HashSet<(StateHash, StateHash, StateHash)>,
}
```

## CoverageMetrics

```rust
pub struct CoverageMetrics {
    schedule: ScheduleCoverage,
    state: StateCoverage,
    race: RaceCoverage,
    transition: TransitionCoverage,

    exploration_time: Duration,
    
    /// Bugs found
    bugs_found: usize,
}

impl CoverageMetrics {
    /// Record a new schedule explored
    fn record_schedule(&mut self, schedule: &NormalizedTrace);
    
    /// Record state visited
    fn record_state(&mut self, state: StateHash);
    
    /// Record race covered
    fn record_race(&mut self, race: &Race, order: RaceOrder);
    
    /// Generate coverage report

}
```

## Coverage Report

```rust
pub struct CoverageReport {

    /// Uncovered items (if identifiable)

    recommendations: Vec<Recommendation>,

pub struct CoverageSummary {
    schedules: String,      // '1,234 / ~10,000 (12.3%)'
    states: String,         // '567 distinct states'
    races: String,          // '23 races, 20 fully covered'
    transitions: String,    // '456 unique transitions'

pub enum Recommendation {
    /// Need more exploration time

    ExploreRace { race: Race, missing_order: RaceOrder },
    
    /// Unexplored state region
    UnexploredRegion { hint: String },
}
```

## Progress Tracking

```rust
pub trait CoverageProgress {
    /// Called periodically during exploration
    fn on_progress(&mut self, metrics: &CoverageMetrics);
    
    /// Called when new coverage achieved
    fn on_new_coverage(&mut self, kind: CoverageKind, details: &str);
}

pub struct ProgressPrinter;
impl CoverageProgress for ProgressPrinter {
    fn on_progress(&mut self, metrics: &CoverageMetrics) {
```

Detect when exploration is 'done enough':

```rust
impl ConvergenceDetector {

        // No new states in last N schedules?
        // Coverage rate below threshold?
    }
}
```

## State Fingerprinting

Efficient state comparison:
```rust
pub type StateHash = u64;

pub trait StateFingerprint {
    fn fingerprint(&self) -> StateHash;
}

impl StateFingerprint for RuntimeState {
    fn fingerprint(&self) -> StateHash {
        // Hash relevant state components
```

-{"id":"bd-2m9k","title":"Reactor trait unification: common interface for all platform backends","description":"# Reactor Trait Unification

## Overview
Define a common Reactor trait that abstracts over io_uring, epoll, kqueue, and IOCP,
allowing platform-agnostic I/O driver implementation.

## Design

### Core Trait
```rust
/// Interest types for I/O operations.
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
pub struct Interest {
    pub readable: bool,
    pub writable: bool,
    pub error: bool,
}

/// Event returned from poll.
pub struct Event {
    pub token: usize,
    pub ready: Interest,
}

/// Collection of events.
pub struct Events {
    inner: Vec<Event>,
    capacity: usize,
}

/// Unified reactor trait.
pub trait Reactor: Send + Sync + 'static {
    /// Register a source for the given interest.
    fn register(
        &self,
        fd: RawFd,
        token: usize,
        interest: Interest,
    ) -> io::Result<()>;
    
    /// Update interest for an already-registered source.
    fn reregister(
        &self,
        fd: RawFd,
        token: usize,
        interest: Interest,
    ) -> io::Result<()>;
    
    /// Remove a source from the reactor.
    fn deregister(&self, fd: RawFd) -> io::Result<()>;
    
    /// Wait for events with optional timeout.
    fn poll(
        &self,
        events: &mut Events,
        timeout: Option<Duration>,
    ) -> io::Result<usize>;
    
    /// Wake up a blocked poll() call.
    fn wake(&self) -> io::Result<()>;
}
```

### Platform Abstraction
```rust
#[cfg(unix)]
type RawSource = std::os::unix::io::RawFd;

#[cfg(windows)]
type RawSource = std::os::windows::io::RawHandle;

/// Source trait for platform-specific handles.
pub trait Source {
    fn raw(&self) -> RawSource;
}
```

### Reactor Factory
```rust
/// Create the best available reactor for the current platform.
pub fn create_reactor() -> io::Result<Arc<dyn Reactor>> {
    #[cfg(all(target_os = "linux", feature = "io-uring"))]
    if io_uring::is_available() {
        return Ok(Arc::new(IoUringReactor::new()?));
    }
    
    #[cfg(target_os = "linux")]
    return Ok(Arc::new(EpollReactor::new()?));
    
    #[cfg(target_os = "macos")]
    return Ok(Arc::new(KqueueReactor::new()?));
    
    #[cfg(target_os = "windows")]
    return Ok(Arc::new(IocpReactor::new()?));
}
```

- IoDriver now holds Arc<dyn Reactor>

+{"id":"bd-2m9k","title":"Reactor trait unification: common interface for all platform backends","description":"# Reactor Trait Unification

## Overview
Define a common Reactor trait that abstracts over io_uring, epoll, kqueue, and IOCP,
allowing platform-agnostic I/O driver implementation.

## Design

### Core Trait
```rust
/// Interest types for I/O operations.
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
pub struct Interest {
    pub readable: bool,
    pub writable: bool,
    pub error: bool,
}

/// Event returned from poll.
pub struct Event {
    pub token: usize,
    pub ready: Interest,
}

/// Collection of events.
pub struct Events {
    inner: Vec<Event>,
    capacity: usize,
}

/// Unified reactor trait.
pub trait Reactor: Send + Sync + 'static {
    /// Register a source for the given interest.
    fn register(
        &self,
        fd: RawFd,
        token: usize,
        interest: Interest,
    ) -> io::Result<()>;
    
    /// Update interest for an already-registered source.
    fn reregister(
        &self,
        fd: RawFd,
        token: usize,
        interest: Interest,
    ) -> io::Result<()>;
    
    /// Remove a source from the reactor.
    fn deregister(&self, fd: RawFd) -> io::Result<()>;
    
    /// Wait for events with optional timeout.
    fn poll(
        &self,
        events: &mut Events,
        timeout: Option<Duration>,
    ) -> io::Result<usize>;
    
    /// Wake up a blocked poll() call.
    fn wake(&self) -> io::Result<()>;
}
```

### Platform Abstraction
```rust
#[cfg(unix)]
type RawSource = std::os::unix::io::RawFd;

#[cfg(windows)]
type RawSource = std::os::windows::io::RawHandle;

/// Source trait for platform-specific handles.
pub trait Source {
    fn raw(&self) -> RawSource;
}
```

### Reactor Factory
```rust
/// Create the best available reactor for the current platform.
pub fn create_reactor() -> io::Result<Arc<dyn Reactor>> {
    #[cfg(all(target_os = "linux", feature = "io-uring"))]
    if io_uring::is_available() {
        return Ok(Arc::new(IoUringReactor::new()?));
    }
    
    #[cfg(target_os = "linux")]
    return Ok(Arc::new(EpollReactor::new()?));
    
    #[cfg(target_os = "macos")]
    return Ok(Arc::new(KqueueReactor::new()?));
    
    #[cfg(target_os = "windows")]
    return Ok(Arc::new(IocpReactor::new()?));
}
```

## Migration from Current io_uring-only Design
- Refactor IoUringReactor to implement Reactor trait
- IoDriver now holds Arc<dyn Reactor>
- All existing code continues to work unchanged

### Queries
```rust
impl PgConnection {
    /// Execute simple query (text protocol).
    pub async fn query(&mut self, cx: &Cx, sql: &str) -> Outcome<Vec<Row>, PgError> {
        cx.checkpoint()?;
        
        // Send Query message
        self.send(Message::Query(sql)).await?;
        
        // Receive RowDescription, DataRow*, CommandComplete, ReadyForQuery
        let mut rows = Vec::new();
        loop {
            match self.recv(cx).await? {
                Message::RowDescription(desc) => { /* parse column info */ }
                Message::DataRow(data) => rows.push(Row::from_data(data)),
                Message::CommandComplete(_) => {}
                Message::ReadyForQuery(_) => break,
                Message::ErrorResponse(e) => return Err(e.into()),
                _ => {}
            }
        }
        
        Ok(rows)
    }
    
    /// Execute extended query (binary protocol).
    pub async fn execute(
        &mut self,
        cx: &Cx,
        sql: &str,
        params: &[&dyn ToSql],
    ) -> Outcome<u64, PgError> {
        cx.checkpoint()?;
        
        // Parse, Bind, Describe, Execute, Sync
        self.send(Message::Parse { name: "", query: sql, param_types: &[] }).await?;
        self.send(Message::Bind { /* ... */ }).await?;
        self.send(Message::Execute { portal: "", max_rows: 0 }).await?;
        self.send(Message::Sync).await?;
        
        // Handle responses
        let mut affected = 0;
        loop {
            match self.recv(cx).await? {
                Message::BindComplete => {}
                Message::CommandComplete(tag) => affected = parse_affected(&tag),
                Message::ReadyForQuery(_) => break,
                Message::ErrorResponse(e) => return Err(e.into()),
                _ => {}
            }
        }
        
        Ok(affected)
    }
}
```

### Transactions
```rust
impl PgConnection {
    /// Begin transaction.
    pub async fn begin(&mut self, cx: &Cx) -> Outcome<Transaction<'_>, PgError> {
        self.query(cx, "BEGIN").await?;
        Ok(Transaction { conn: self, committed: false })
    }
}

pub struct Transaction<'a> {
    conn: &'a mut PgConnection,
    committed: bool,
}

impl<'a> Transaction<'a> {
    /// Commit transaction.
    pub async fn commit(mut self, cx: &Cx) -> Outcome<(), PgError> {
        self.conn.query(cx, "COMMIT").await?;
        self.committed = true;
        Ok(())
    }
    
    /// Rollback transaction.
    pub async fn rollback(mut self, cx: &Cx) -> Outcome<(), PgError> {
        self.conn.query(cx, "ROLLBACK").await?;
        self.committed = true;
        Ok(())
    }
}

impl<'a> Drop for Transaction<'a> {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        if !self.committed {
            // Log warning: transaction not committed/rolled back
            // Will be auto-rolled back on connection close
        }
    }
}
```
